behaviors.description: Command segmentation treats newline-separated statements as separate segments, not one monolithic block.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the segmentation logic itself (single parser/splitter responsibility) with no required I/O. Adversarial critique: if segmentation only fails when classifier consumes segments, Integration could be needed; rejected because behavior text is explicitly about segmentation output, not downstream handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Call the segmentation function with a multi-line shell snippet containing assignments and a command, and assert it returns distinct ordered segments per statement rather than one combined segment.

behaviors.description: A multi-line shell block with variable assignments followed by `gh pr create` is recognized as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Real failure came from module interaction: segmenter output fed into trigger classifier produced `not_a_trigger`. Unit tests of either module alone can miss this handoff bug (MOCK-MISS). Integration-brake checks: no real network/OS/subprocess required for detection itself; no AI/ML dependence; no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real segmentation + trigger-detection modules, feed a multi-line block (`VAR=...` lines then `gh pr create ...`), and assert final classification is PR-create trigger.

behaviors.description: A similar multi-line block that ends with `gh issue create` is not incorrectly treated as a PR-create trigger.
behaviors.justification: Negative classification depends on the same cross-module path (segmentation + trigger matching) and guards against over-broad trigger logic. Adversarial critique: could be Unit if matcher alone distinguishes `pr` vs `issue`; rejected because behavior explicitly keeps the multi-line structure that previously broke classification at module boundaries.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end local classification path on a multi-line block with assignments plus `gh issue create`; assert result is not PR-create trigger.

behaviors.description: When a PR-create trigger is detected, the hook can reconstruct and persist the PR identifier needed by the review loop state.
behaviors.justification: Requires parser/extractor + state-writer interaction and filesystem persistence. A pure Unit test of extraction or serialization alone may miss contract mismatch or write-path issues. Rejected System because behavior text does not require real subprocess/network/OS-specific primitives beyond local file persistence.
behaviors.test_description: Using a temp directory, execute detection->identifier reconstruction->state write flow and assert persisted state contains the expected PR identifier fields consumed by review loop logic.

behaviors.description: After PR-create detection, the review gate state is written in a form that the pre-tool enforcement hook can read and enforce.
behaviors.justification: This is a producer/consumer contract test across two local modules/hooks via shared state file (handoff failure boundary). Adversarial critique: might be System if true hook runtime process/env semantics are required; rejected because behavior text focuses on readable/enforceable state format, which is testable by invoking writer then reader/enforcer logic locally.
behaviors.test_description: Run PostToolUse state writer on a detected PR-create event, then invoke pre-tool enforcement reader against that written state and assert enforcement activates as expected.
